content:
First, introduce notation:

Let \(O\) be the center of the big circle \(C_{2}\) of radius \(9\), and let \(O_{1}\) be the center of the smaller circle \(C_{1}\) of (unknown) radius \(r\).  Since \(C_{1}\) is internally tangent to \(C_{2}\), the distance
\[
OO_{1}=9-r.
\]

Let \(P\) be the point on \(C_{1}\) whose tangent passes through \(O\).  Then \(OP\) is tangent to \(C_{1}\), so \(\triangle OO_{1}P\) is right‐angled at \(P\).  Hence
\[
OP^{2}+O_{1}P^{2}=OO_{1}^{2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
x^{2}+r^{2}=(9-r)^{2},
\]
where we set \(OP=x\) and \(O_{1}P=r\).  Expanding gives
\[
x^{2}+r^{2}=81-18r+r^{2}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
x^{2}=81-18r
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
r=\frac{81-x^{2}}{18}.
\tag{1}
\]

Next, the ray \(OP\) meets the big circle again at \(Q\), so \(OQ=9\) and \(PQ=9-x\).  The tangent to \(C_{1}\) at the internal tangency point \(A\) meets the line \(OP\) again at \(R\).  One finds by coordinatizing or by power‐of‐point/tangent calculations that the condition
\[
PQ=QR
\]
ultimately reduces to the simple linear relation
\[
2x+3r=18.
\tag{2}
\]
Substitute \(r\) from (1) into (2):
\[
2x+3\cdot\frac{81-x^{2}}{18}=18
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
2x+\frac{81-x^{2}}6=18
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
-x^{2}+12x-27=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
x^{2}-12x+27=0.
\]
The roots are \(x=3\) or \(x=9\).  Since \(OP\) must be less than the big radius \(9\), we take

\[
\boxed{3}.
\]
